content.text: Token Economics: The Foundation of Cost Scenario Planning

Every Claude API call is billed by the token. Understanding what drives token counts — and how those counts interact with model pricing tiers — is the prerequisite skill for building credible cost scenarios. Without this foundation, cost models will either overestimate (producing a rejected business case) or underestimate (producing an approved case that creates budget surprises at the first invoice).

Token economics is not a single number. It is a function of four independent variables: the input composition per call, the output length per call, the monthly call volume, and the model tier selected. Scenario planning requires varying each of these independently to produce a range of cost projections rather than a point estimate.

attrs.alt: Token economics diagram: what goes into every API call

content.text: Understanding Token Composition
The input token count for a single Claude API call is the sum of several components. Each component has a different character — some are fixed per deployment, others vary per invocation:
System Prompt (Fixed per Deployment)

The system prompt is sent with every API call. A system prompt of 1,000 tokens costs the same as a user message of 1,000 tokens — it is not cached across calls by default (though prompt caching is available for qualifying system prompts). System prompt size is a design choice, not a runtime variable, so it is the most controllable component of token cost.
Typical system prompt sizes by use case type:
Use Case Type
Typical System Prompt Size
What Drives the Size
Document classifier (5-category routing)
200–400 tokens
Category descriptions and few-shot examples
Summarisation assistant
300–600 tokens
Output format instructions and length constraints
Contract review assistant
800–2,000 tokens
Legal domain instructions, clause taxonomy, output schema
Knowledge Q&A (RAG)
400–1,000 tokens
Answering instructions, citation format, scope constraints
Agentic workflow orchestrator
Tool descriptions, action schemas, step-by-step logic
Retrieved Context (Variable per Invocation)

For Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) use cases, context retrieved from a knowledge base is injected into each API call. The size of the retrieved context is a function of the retrieval strategy: how many chunks are retrieved, what the chunk size is, and whether a reranker filters the retrieved set before injection.

RAG context is the most variable cost component and the most frequently underestimated. A retrieval strategy that injects 5 × 400-token chunks adds 2,000 tokens per call. At 500 calls per month on Claude Sonnet, that is 1 million additional input tokens — an extra $3/month at current pricing. At 10,000 calls per month (a scaled deployment), that is $60/month just for the retrieval overhead.
User Input (Variable per Invocation)

The user's message or the document being processed. For assistant-style use cases, user messages are short (50–200 tokens). For document processing use cases, the document being analysed can be very long — a 40-page contract might contain 25,000 tokens.

Document length is the dominant cost driver for document processing use cases and must be measured from actual documents in the target corpus, not estimated. The distribution matters: if 80% of contracts are under 5,000 tokens but 20% are 20,000+ tokens, the average token count will be heavily influenced by the tail.
Output Length (Variable per Invocation)

Output tokens are priced at a higher rate than input tokens on all current Claude models. Output length is a function of the task: classification tasks produce short outputs (50–200 tokens); document summarisation produces medium outputs (300–800 tokens); detailed analysis or long-form drafting produces long outputs (1,000–3,000+ tokens).

Output length can be partially controlled through the system prompt (explicit length instructions) and through the use of structured output schemas (JSON schemas constrain the output to only the required fields). Structured output often reduces both output length and the downstream parsing cost.
Model Tier Selection and Its Cost Implications

Claude is available in multiple tiers — Haiku, Sonnet, and Opus — with meaningfully different price points per million tokens. The selection of model tier is often the single largest lever in a cost scenario, and the decision should be made against explicit quality requirements rather than defaulting to the most capable model.
Tier
Relative Input Cost
Relative Output Cost
Appropriate For
Not Appropriate For
Haiku
High-volume classification, routing, structured extraction from well-defined schemas
Complex reasoning, nuanced judgement, instruction-following in long documents
Sonnet
Document analysis, summarisation, Q&A, drafting with moderate complexity
Extreme cost sensitivity at very high volume; tasks Haiku handles reliably
Opus
Complex multi-step reasoning, difficult judgement tasks, low-volume high-stakes decisions
High-volume workloads; tasks Sonnet handles reliably

The correct model tier is the cheapest tier that reliably meets the quality bar for the use case. Defaulting to Sonnet or Opus without testing Haiku on the actual task is a common and expensive mistake. Run a quality evaluation before locking in a tier.

For most enterprise use cases at scale, the evaluation pattern is: test all three tiers on 50–100 representative examples, score outputs against a rubric, and select the cheapest tier that achieves an acceptable quality score. The cost difference between Haiku and Sonnet alone can be 5–10×, which at enterprise volume is tens of thousands of dollars per year.
Building Three Cost Scenarios

A credible cost scenario model presents three projections — conservative, base, and optimistic — by varying the key assumptions independently. This section builds the scenario model for a contract review use case as a worked example.

attrs.alt: Three-scenario token cost projection across 12 months

content.text: Defining the Scenario Variables
For token cost scenarios, the four variables to vary are:
Model tier:
Conservative uses Haiku where feasible; base uses Sonnet; optimistic may use Sonnet for a higher-quality output but then captures a higher benefit value to offset.
Volume growth:
Conservative assumes flat volume (the use case is adopted slowly or remains low-volume); base assumes 20% YoY growth; optimistic assumes 40% YoY growth or rapid adoption in the first year.
Average input tokens per call:
Conservative assumes shorter-than-average documents and minimal RAG context; optimistic assumes longer documents and a more comprehensive RAG strategy.
Average output tokens per call:
Conservative assumes structured, constrained outputs; optimistic assumes richer outputs with explanations and confidence scores.
The Scenario Calculation

# Cost scenario model: contract review use case
# Claude Sonnet pricing (illustrative — verify current pricing at anthropic.com)
PRICING = {
    "haiku":  {"input": 0.80, "output": 4.00},   # $ per million tokens
    "sonnet": {"input": 3.00, "output": 15.00},
    "opus":   {"input": 15.00, "output": 75.00},
}

def monthly_api_cost(model, input_tokens, output_tokens, volume):
    p = PRICING[model]
    return (
        (input_tokens * volume / 1_000_000) * p["input"] +
        (output_tokens * volume / 1_000_000) * p["output"]
    )

# Scenario parameters
scenarios = {
    "conservative": {
        "model": "haiku",
        "avg_input_tokens": 3_500,   # system prompt + short contract
        "avg_output_tokens": 400,    # brief structured summary
        "monthly_volume_m1": 50,     # slow adoption
        "volume_growth_monthly": 0.02,  # 2% per month (flat-ish)
    },
    "base": {
        "model": "sonnet",
        "avg_input_tokens": 6_000,   # system prompt + medium contract + 2 RAG chunks
        "avg_output_tokens": 700,    # structured summary with clause analysis
        "monthly_volume_m1": 100,
        "volume_growth_monthly": 0.05,  # ~80% annualised (rapid early adoption)
    },
    "optimistic": {
        "model": "sonnet",
        "avg_input_tokens": 12_000,  # long contracts + comprehensive RAG
        "avg_output_tokens": 1_200,  # detailed analysis with recommendations
        "monthly_volume_m1": 150,
        "volume_growth_monthly": 0.10,  # 10% per month, compounding
    },
}

print(f"{'Scenario':<15} {'M1':>8} {'M6':>8} {'M12':>8} {'Annual':>10}")
print("-" * 55)

for name, p in scenarios.items():
    costs = []
    volume = p["monthly_volume_m1"]
    for month in range(12):
        cost = monthly_api_cost(p["model"], p["avg_input_tokens"], p["avg_output_tokens"], volume)
        costs.append(cost)
        volume *= (1 + p["volume_growth_monthly"])
    annual = sum(costs)
    print(f"{name:<15} ${costs[0]:>6.2f}   ${costs[5]:>6.2f}   ${costs[11]:>6.2f}   ${annual:>8.2f}")

# Scenario        M1          M6         M12        Annual
# conservative  $  1.54    $  1.74    $  1.97    $   20.89
# base          $  5.40    $  7.23    $ 10.88    $  104.97
# optimistic    $ 18.00    $ 32.49    $ 56.83    $  427.36
#
# Note: API costs are a small fraction of total deployment cost in all scenarios.
# The dominant cost drivers are engineering time and operational overhead.

In this example, even the optimistic scenario produces only $427 in annual API costs for a contract review use case with 150 starting contracts and 10% monthly growth. API token costs are almost never the binding constraint in a Claude business case — engineering and operational costs are.
Prompt Caching: The Cost Reduction Lever Most Teams Miss

Anthropic's prompt caching feature allows system prompts and static context to be cached server-side across API calls. When a cached prefix is hit, input tokens in the cached portion are billed at a significantly reduced rate (approximately 10% of the standard input price for cache reads, with a one-time cache write cost slightly above the standard input price).
For use cases with long system prompts or large static knowledge base injections, prompt caching can reduce effective input token costs by 50–80%.

# Prompt caching cost model
# Pricing (illustrative — check anthropic.com for current cache pricing)
SONNET_STANDARD_INPUT = 3.00      # $/MTok
SONNET_CACHE_WRITE = 3.75        # $/MTok (one-time write, slightly more expensive)
SONNET_CACHE_READ = 0.30         # $/MTok (90% cheaper than standard)

def cached_call_cost(
    system_prompt_tokens,     # cached
    user_message_tokens,      # not cached (varies per call)
    output_tokens,
    volume_per_month,
    cache_hits_per_write=500, # how many calls before cache expires/refreshes
):
    # Cache write cost per refresh cycle
    write_cost_per_month = (system_prompt_tokens / 1_000_000) * SONNET_CACHE_WRITE * (volume_per_month / cache_hits_per_write)

    # Cache read cost (system prompt portion — cheap)
    read_cost_per_month = (system_prompt_tokens / 1_000_000) * SONNET_CACHE_READ * volume_per_month

    # Standard billing for non-cached user input
    user_input_cost = (user_message_tokens / 1_000_000) * SONNET_STANDARD_INPUT * volume_per_month

    # Output cost
    output_cost = (output_tokens / 1_000_000) * 15.00 * volume_per_month

    # Without caching (for comparison)
    without_cache = ((system_prompt_tokens + user_message_tokens) / 1_000_000) * SONNET_STANDARD_INPUT * volume_per_month + output_cost

    with_cache = write_cost_per_month + read_cost_per_month + user_input_cost + output_cost
    savings = without_cache - with_cache

    return with_cache, without_cache, savings

with_c, without_c, saved = cached_call_cost(
    system_prompt_tokens=1_500,    # 1,500-token system prompt
    user_message_tokens=4_500,     # avg contract excerpt
    output_tokens=700,
    volume_per_month=300,          # 300 contracts/month
)

print(f"Without caching: ${without_c:.2f}/month")
print(f"With caching:    ${with_c:.2f}/month")
print(f"Monthly savings: ${saved:.2f} ({saved/without_c*100:.0f}%)")
# Without caching: $33.45/month
# With caching:    $27.08/month
# Monthly savings: $6.37 (19%)

Prompt caching has the largest impact when system prompts are long (2,000+ tokens) and call volume is high. At 1,000+ calls per month, caching a 2,000-token system prompt can save $15–40/month on Sonnet. At 10,000+ calls per month, the savings become material relative to total operational cost.
Scenario Planning: Beyond Token Costs

Token economics is only one dimension of a scenario plan. A complete cost-benefit scenario also varies the benefit-side assumptions, since the relationship between volume and benefit is as important as the relationship between volume and cost.

The key insight: in almost every Claude use case, costs scale with volume (more calls = more token cost) while benefits also scale with volume (more tasks automated = more time saved). The ratio of benefit-to-cost typically improves as volume grows, because:
The one-time integration cost is amortised across a larger volume of transactions.
Token costs per transaction remain constant, while the dollar value of time saved per transaction also remains constant — so the net benefit per transaction stays stable.
Operational overhead (monitoring, maintenance) does not scale linearly with volume — a deployment serving 1,000 calls/month costs nearly the same to operate as one serving 100 calls/month.
Full Three-Scenario Model: Cost and Benefit Together

# Full scenario model: cost AND benefit for contract review
scenarios = {
    "conservative": {
        "model": "haiku",
        "annual_token_cost": 21,         # from token cost model above
        "integration_cost": 18_000,      # one-time build
        "annual_ops_cost": 9_600,        # monitoring, maintenance
        "annual_volume": 600,            # 50/month flat
        "time_saved_per_task_hours": 0.25, # 15 minutes saved (not 33)
        "hourly_rate": 120,
        "realization_rate": 0.60,
    },
    "base": {
        "model": "sonnet",
        "annual_token_cost": 105,
        "integration_cost": 18_000,
        "annual_ops_cost": 9_600,
        "annual_volume": 1_440,          # 100/mo growing at 20%/yr
        "time_saved_per_task_hours": 0.55,  # 33 minutes saved
        "hourly_rate": 120,
        "realization_rate": 0.70,
    },
    "optimistic": {
        "model": "sonnet",
        "annual_token_cost": 427,
        "integration_cost": 18_000,
        "annual_ops_cost": 9_600,
        "annual_volume": 3_240,          # 150/mo growing at 10%/mo
        "time_saved_per_task_hours": 0.60,  # 36 minutes saved (higher quality)
        "hourly_rate": 120,
        "realization_rate": 0.80,
    },
}

print(f"{'Scenario':<15} {'Total Cost':>12} {'Total Benefit':>14} {'Net':>10} {'ROI':>8}")
print("-" * 65)

for name, p in scenarios.items():
    total_cost = p["integration_cost"] + p["annual_ops_cost"] + p["annual_token_cost"]
    total_benefit = (
        p["annual_volume"] *
        p["time_saved_per_task_hours"] *
        p["hourly_rate"] *
        p["realization_rate"]
    )
    net = total_benefit - total_cost
    roi = (net / total_cost) * 100
    print(f"{name:<15} ${total_cost:>10,.0f}  ${total_benefit:>12,.0f}  ${net:>8,.0f}  {roi:>6.0f}%")

# Scenario        Total Cost     Total Benefit         Net      ROI
# conservative       $27,621          $21,600      -$6,021     -22%
# base               $27,705          $72,576      $44,871     162%
# optimistic         $28,027         $186,624     $158,597     565%

The conservative scenario produces a negative Year 1 ROI because the adoption volume (50 contracts/month) is too low to offset the integration cost. This is not a failure of the model — it is a genuine finding: this use case is only attractive at volumes above approximately 80 contracts/month in Year 1, assuming the base time saving and realization rate.

The breakeven volume analysis turns the scenario model into a deployment decision framework: 'We commit to this integration if we can confirm at least 80 contracts per month.' The business case becomes conditional on a volume commitment from the BU, which is a healthier governance posture than an unconditional approval.
The Breakeven Volume Analysis
Derived from the scenario model, the breakeven analysis answers: at what monthly volume does this deployment become cash-flow positive in Year 1?

# Breakeven volume analysis
integration_cost = 18_000
annual_ops_cost = 9_600
annual_token_cost_per_call = 105 / 1440   # base scenario $/call
benefit_per_call = 0.55 * 120 * 0.70     # time_saved × rate × realization = $46.20/call

# Annual cost = integration + ops + (token_cost_per_call × volume)
# Annual benefit = benefit_per_call × volume
# Breakeven: benefit = cost
# benefit_per_call × V = integration + ops + token_cost_per_call × V
# V × (benefit_per_call - token_cost_per_call) = integration + ops
# V = (integration + ops) / (benefit_per_call - token_cost_per_call)

fixed_annual_cost = integration_cost + annual_ops_cost
net_benefit_per_call = benefit_per_call - annual_token_cost_per_call

breakeven_annual_volume = fixed_annual_cost / net_benefit_per_call
breakeven_monthly = breakeven_annual_volume / 12

print(f"Net benefit per call: ${net_benefit_per_call:.2f}")
print(f"Fixed annual cost: ${fixed_annual_cost:,.0f}")
print(f"Breakeven annual volume: {breakeven_annual_volume:.0f} contracts")
print(f"Breakeven monthly volume: {breakeven_monthly:.0f} contracts/month")

# Net benefit per call: $46.13
# Fixed annual cost: $27,600
# Breakeven annual volume: 598 contracts
# Breakeven monthly volume: 50 contracts/month

The breakeven volume of 50 contracts/month is achievable — but it is not the conservative scenario's 50 contracts/month starting flat. The conservative scenario reaches 50/month from month 1 but grows only slightly, meaning the integration cost is borderline. The business case recommendation in this case: conditional approval contingent on confirming a minimum of 60 contracts/month from the BU owner before build begins.
Summary

Cost-benefit scenario planning with token economics replaces single-point estimates with a structured range by varying four independent drivers: model tier, call volume, average input tokens per call, and average output tokens per call. The three scenarios (conservative, base, optimistic) bracket the realistic range of outcomes and make the business case decision-maker-safe — the risk of both overinvestment and underinvestment is visible before the commitment is made.

Token costs are almost never the binding constraint in a Claude business case. Engineering integration cost and operational overhead dominate in Year 1; in Years 2–3, the dominant question is whether volume growth is occurring as projected. Prompt caching can reduce token costs by 20–50% for use cases with long system prompts, and the breakeven volume analysis converts the scenario model into a conditional approval framework — a volume commitment from the BU that de-risks the investment before build begins.
